theory.intro: Unit fractions all have a numerator of $1$. The **larger the denominator, the smaller the fraction**, so they can be ordered just by their denominators.
facts: On a number line from $0$ to $1$, $\tfrac{1}{n}$ sits $\tfrac{1}{n}$ of the way along.
To order, compare the **denominators** — the largest denominator gives the smallest fraction.
steps: Order the denominators (smallest denominator = largest fraction).
Write the fractions in the matching order.

foundation.q: Which point is $\tfrac{1}{3}$ on the line?

development.q: Place $\tfrac{1}{5}$ and $\tfrac{1}{2}$ on a $0$ to $1$ line and state which is closer to $0$.
Insert the missing unit fraction between $\tfrac{1}{4}$ and $\tfrac{1}{2}$ on the line.
Three runners finish $\tfrac{1}{2}$, $\tfrac{1}{3}$ and $\tfrac{1}{8}$ of a track. Order the distances, shortest first.
Which unit fractions lie between $\tfrac{1}{6}$ and $\tfrac{1}{2}$? (denominators $2$–$6$)
On a number line, $A$ is at $\tfrac{1}{4}$ and $B$ at $\tfrac{1}{8}$. Which is closer to $\tfrac{1}{2}$?
True or false: on a number line $\tfrac{1}{10}$ is closer to $0$ than $\tfrac{1}{3}$.
Five cups hold $\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{4}, \tfrac{1}{5}, \tfrac{1}{10}$ of a litre. List from most to least.

mastery.q: Without converting to decimals, order $\tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{8}, \tfrac{1}{5}, \tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{10}$ smallest first and justify.
A unit fraction lies exactly halfway between $\tfrac{1}{2}$ and $0$ on the line. Which is it?
Three pizzas the same size are cut into $4$, $6$ and $10$ slices. Which single slice is largest, and order all three slices.
Liam says $\tfrac{1}{5} > \tfrac{1}{3}$ because $5 > 3$. Explain his error.
mastery.a: Bigger denominator gives a smaller fraction, so $\tfrac{1}{5} < \tfrac{1}{3}$

mastery.q: Which unit fraction is closest to $\tfrac{1}{2}$ but not equal to it: $\tfrac{1}{3}$ or $\tfrac{1}{4}$?
On a $0$–$1$ line, mark where $\tfrac{1}{6}, \tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{2}$ go, then state the gaps in sixths.
Four taps fill $\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{4}, \tfrac{1}{8}, \tfrac{1}{3}$ of a tank per minute. Order them by speed, fastest first.
Find a unit fraction larger than $\tfrac{1}{10}$ but smaller than $\tfrac{1}{8}$.
Explain, using a number line, why the gap from $\tfrac{1}{2}$ to $\tfrac{1}{3}$ is bigger than the gap from $\tfrac{1}{4}$ to $\tfrac{1}{5}$.
mastery.a: Smaller denominators are spread further apart

mastery.q: A list of unit fractions is given as $\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{5}, \tfrac{1}{4}, \tfrac{1}{10}, \tfrac{1}{8}$. Which is the median (middle value when ordered)?
